original_abstract: This article examines the exposition of the first commandment within the Heidelberg Catechism (HC), providing reconstructed versions of the original German and Latin texts. Given that Zacharias Ursinus, the primary author of the HC, was a student of Philipp Melanchthon in Wittenberg, two of Melanchthon’s seminal publications are analyzed to establish the theological framework and intellectual context of the manual. Furthermore, the study explores Ursinus’s own interpretations of the first commandment as found in his Small and Large Catechisms, alongside insights from his dogmatic lectures. By synthesising these sources, the article seeks to offer a more nuanced and comprehensive understanding of the first commandment's placement and meaning within the Heidelberg Catechism.
humanized_abstract: The First Commandment in the Heidelberg Catechism This article looks at how the first commandment is explained in the Heidelberg Catechism (HC). For each Dutch question and statement, the reconstructed German and Latin versions are provided. Since Zacharias Ursinus, the main author of the HC, was a student of Philipp Melanchthon in Wittenberg, two of Melanchthon’s influential treatises are examined to lay the groundwork for understanding the theological and exgetical matter underlying the Catechism. In addition, the article will also take a closer look at Zacharias Ursinus’ understanding of the first commandment in his Small and Large Catechisms as well as in his dogmatics lectures. In combination, these elements should provide insight into the reasons why the first commandment was placed where it is in the Heidelberg Catechism.
